critic_raw:
Two critical flaws.

Section 3 explicitly states: 'All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting.' This is comprehensive preprocessing leakage. TF-IDF IDF weights, z-score scaling parameters for lab values, and radiological image normalization statistics are all computed using the held-out test patients. For a 24-month clinical dataset, TF-IDF IDF weights are particularly impactful—they encode document frequency distributions that differ meaningfully between a 70% training sample and the full corpus, especially as clinical language evolves with new treatment guidelines and diagnostic codes.

The split strategy uses 'stratified random sample across all months' — 70/15/15. For a 24-month hospital dataset, the same patient may have multiple disease episodes. No patient-level split enforcement is specified. For patients with chronic conditions (hypertension, diabetes, heart failure), multiple episodes per year are expected, making patient-level overlap a significant concern.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting — explicit comprehensive leakage
TF-IDF IDF weights non-trivially affected by test-set inclusion — evolving clinical language leakage
No patient-level split enforcement in longitudinal 24-month clinical dataset

round_transcripts.critic: Two critical flaws: (1) All preprocessing transformers explicitly fit on entire dataset — comprehensive leakage across TF-IDF, lab scaling, image normalization per Section 3. (2) Random split on 24-month longitudinal clinical data — same patient episodes across splits without patient-level enforcement.
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing: training is 70% (~16-17 months) of the full dataset. Training-only preprocessing on this large proportion yields nearly identical parameters to full-dataset fitting for stable clinical variables. TF-IDF vocabulary differences between 70% and 100% are minimal when the clinical domain vocabulary is standardized.

On patient splitting: the design aligns records by patient identifier and each record is a clinical episode. For many disease conditions, patients contribute one episode per episode type. Stratification by class ensures disease label distribution is consistent.

round_transcripts.critic:
On TF-IDF: IDF weights depend on document frequency across the corpus. Adding 30% more documents changes the IDF weights for all terms. Terms that are common in the test period but rare in training get artificially lower IDF weights, distorting feature representations. For a 24-month clinical dataset where language evolves with changing treatment guidelines and new medications, this is meaningful—not minimal.

On patient splitting: the defense assumes one episode per patient per episode type. This is not stated in the design and is almost certainly incorrect for a disease diagnosis dataset over 24 months with chronic conditions. No patient-level enforcement is specified.
round_transcripts.defender:
I concede that TF-IDF IDF weights are non-trivially affected by including test-set documents. Unlike simple scaling statistics, IDF weights encode document frequency distributions that can meaningfully differ for clinical text over 24 months.

On patient splitting: the lack of explicit patient-level split enforcement is a real gap for a disease diagnosis dataset with chronic conditions.

all_issues_adjudicated: TF-IDF IDF weights and image normalization fit on full dataset — meaningful leakage for evolving clinical language and imaging
No patient-level split enforcement in longitudinal 24-month clinical dataset
